CSE5313CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 21)

CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 21)

Gradient coding

Intro to Statistical Machine Learning

Given by the learning problem:

Unknown target function f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y.

Training data D={(xi,yi)}i=1n\mathcal{D}=\{(x_i,y_i)\}_{i=1}^nD={(xi​,yi​)}i=1n​.

Learning algorithm: A\mathbb{A}A and Hypothesis set H\mathcal{H}H.

Goal is to find g≈fg\approx fg≈f,

Common hypothesis sets:

  • Linear classifiers:
f(x)=sign(wx⊤)f(x)=sign (wx^\top)f(x)=sign(wx⊤)
  • Linear regressors:
f(x)=wx⊤f(x)=wx^\topf(x)=wx⊤
  • Neural networks:
    • Concatenated linear classifiers (or differentiable approximation thereof).

The dataset D\mathcal{D}D is taken from unknown distribution DDD.

Common approach – Empirical Risk Minimization

  • Q: How to quantify f≈gf\approx gf≈g ?
  • A: Use a loss function.
    • A function which measures the deviation between ggg‘s output and fff‘s output.
  • Using the chosen loss function, define two measures:
    • True risk: ED[L(f,g)]\mathbb{E}_{D}[\mathbb{L}(f,g)]ED​[L(f,g)]
    • Empirical risk: 1n∑i=1nℓ(g(xi),yi)\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \ell(g(x_i),y_i)n1​∑i=1n​ℓ(g(xi​),yi​)

Machine learning is over R\mathbb{R}R.

Gradient Descent (Motivation)

Parameterize ggg using some real vector w⃗\vec{w}w, we want to minimize ER(w⃗)ER(\vec{w})ER(w).

Algorithm:

  • Initialize w⃗0\vec{w}_0w0​.
  • For t=1,2,⋯ ,Tt=1,2,\cdots,Tt=1,2,⋯,T:
    • Computer ∇w⃗ER(w⃗)\nabla_{\vec{w}} ER(\vec{w})∇w​ER(w).
    • w⃗←w⃗−η∇w⃗ER(w⃗)\vec{w}\gets \vec{w}-\eta\nabla_{\vec{w}} ER(\vec{w})w←w−η∇w​ER(w)
    • Terminate if some stop condition are met.

Bottleneck: Calculating ∇w⃗ER(w⃗)=1n∑i=1n∇w⃗ℓ(g(xi),yi)\nabla_{\vec{w}} ER(\vec{w})=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \nabla_{\vec{w}} \ell(g(x_i),y_i)∇w​ER(w)=n1​∑i=1n​∇w​ℓ(g(xi​),yi​).

Potentially, O(PN)O(PN)O(PN) where NNN is number of points, dimension of feature space.

Solution: Parallelize.

Distributed Gradient Descent

Idea: use a distributed system with master and workers.

Problem: Stragglers (slow servers, 5-6 slower than average time).

Potential Solutions:

  • Wait for all servers:
    • Accurate, but slow
  • Sum results without the slowest ones
    • Less accurate, but faster
  • Introduce redundancy
    • Send each Di\mathcal{D}_iDi​ to more than one server.
    • Each server receives more than one Di\mathcal{D}_iDi​.
    • Each server sends a linear combination of the partials gradient to its Di\mathcal{D}_iDi​.
    • The master decodes the sum of partial gradients from the linear combination.

Problem setups

System setup: 1 master MMM, nnn workers W1,⋯ ,WnW_1,\cdots,W_nW1​,⋯,Wn​.

A dataset D={(xi,yi)}i=1n\mathcal{D}=\{(x_i,y_i)\}_{i=1}^nD={(xi​,yi​)}i=1n​.

Each server jjj:

  • Receives some ddd data points Dj={(xj,i,yj,i)}i=1d\mathcal{D}_j=\{(x_{j,i},y_{j,i})\}_{i=1}^dDj​={(xj,i​,yj,i​)}i=1d​. where ddd is the replication factor.
  • Computes a certain vector viv_ivi​ from each (xj,i,yj,i)(x_{j,i},y_{j,i})(xj,i​,yj,i​).
  • Returns a linear combination ui=∑j=1nαjvju_i=\sum_{j=1}^n \alpha_j v_jui​=∑j=1n​αj​vj​. to the master.

The master:

  • Waits for the first n−sn-sn−s uiu_iui​‘s to arrive (sss is the straggler tolerance factor).
  • Linear combines the uiu_iui​‘s to get the final gradient.

Goal: Retrieve ∑ivi\sum_i v_i∑i​vi​ regardless of which n−sn-sn−s workers responded.

  • Computation of the full gradient that tolerates any sss straggler.

The αi,j\alpha_{i,j}αi,j​‘s are fixed (do not depend on data). These form a gradient coding matrix B∈Cn×nB\in\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}B∈Cn×n.

Row iii has ddd non-zero entries αi,j\alpha_{i,j}αi,j​. at some positions.

The λi\lambda_iλi​‘s:

  • Might depend on the identity of the n−sn-sn−s responses.
  • Nevertheless must exists in any case.

Recall:

  • The master must be able to recover ∑ivi\sum_i v_i∑i​vi​ form any n−sn-sn−s responses.

Let K\mathcal{K}K be the indices of the responses.

Let BKB_\mathcal{K}BK​ be the submatrix of BBB indexed by K\mathcal{K}K.

Must have:

  • For every K\mathcal{K}K of size n−sn-sn−s there exists coefficients λ1,⋯ ,λn−s\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_{n-s}λ1​,⋯,λn−s​ such that:
(λ1,⋯ ,λn−s)BK=(1,1,1,1,⋯ ,1)=I(\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_{n-s})B_\mathcal{K}=(1,1,1,1,\cdots,1)=\mathbb{I}(λ1​,⋯,λn−s​)BK​=(1,1,1,1,⋯,1)=I

Then if K={i1,⋯ ,in−s}\mathcal{K}=\{i_1,\cdots,i_{n-s}\}K={i1​,⋯,in−s​} responded,

(λ1vi1,⋯ ,λn−svin−s)(ui1ui2⋮uin−s)=∑ivi(\lambda_1 v_{i_1},\cdots,\lambda_{n-s} v_{i_{n-s}})\begin{pmatrix} u_{i_1}\\ u_{i_2}\\ \vdots\\ u_{i_{n-s}} \end{pmatrix}=\sum_i v_i(λ1​vi1​​,⋯,λn−s​vin−s​​)​ui1​​ui2​​⋮uin−s​​​​=i∑​vi​

Definition of gradient coding matrix.

For replication factor ddd and straggler tolerance factor sss: B∈Cn×nB\in\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}B∈Cn×n is a gradient coding matrix if:

  • I\mathbb{I}I is in th span of any n−sn-sn−s rows.
  • Every row of BBB contains at most ddd nonzero elements.

Grading coding matrix implies gradient coding algorithm:

  • The master sends SiS_iSi​ to worker iii. where i=1,⋯ ,ni=1,\cdots,ni=1,⋯,n are the nonzero indices of row iii.
  • Worker iii:
    • Computes Di,ℓ→vi,ℓ\mathcal{D}_{i,\ell}\to v_{i,\ell}Di,ℓ​→vi,ℓ​ for ℓ=1,⋯ ,d\ell=1,\cdots,dℓ=1,⋯,d.
    • Sends ui=∑j=1dαi,jvi,ju_i=\sum_{j=1}^d \alpha_{i,j}v_{i,j}ui​=∑j=1d​αi,j​vi,j​ to the master.
  • Let K={i1,⋯ ,in−s}\mathcal{K}=\{i_1,\cdots,i_{n-s}\}K={i1​,⋯,in−s​} be the indices of the first n−sn-sn−s responses.
  • Since I\mathbb{I}I is in the span of any n−sn-sn−s rows of BBB, there exists λ1,⋯ ,λn−s\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_{n-s}λ1​,⋯,λn−s​ such that (λ1,⋯ ,λn−s)((ui1,⋯ ,uin−s))=∑ivi(\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_{n-s})((u_{i_1},\cdots,u_{i_{n-s}}))=\sum_i v_i(λ1​,⋯,λn−s​)((ui1​​,⋯,uin−s​​))=∑i​vi​.

Construction of Gradient Coding Matrices

Goal:

  • For a given straggler tolerance parameter sss, we wish to construct a gradient coding matrix BBB with the smallest possible ddd.

  • Tools:

I. Cyclic Reed-Solomon codes over the complex numbers. II. Definition of C⊥\mathcal{C}^\perpC⊥ (dual of C\mathcal {C}C) and CR\mathcal{C}^RCR (reverse of C\mathcal{C}C). III. A simple lemma about MDS codes.

Recall: An n,kn,kn,k Reed-Solomon code over a field F\mathcal{F}F is as follows.

  • Fix distinct α1,⋯ ,αn−1∈F\alpha_1,\cdots,\alpha_n-1\in\mathcal{F}α1​,⋯,αn​−1∈F.
  • C={f(α1),f(α2),⋯ ,f(αn−1)}\mathcal{C}=\{f(\alpha_1),f(\alpha_2),\cdots,f(\alpha_n-1)\}C={f(α1​),f(α2​),⋯,f(αn​−1)}.
  • Dimension kkk and minimum distance n−k+1n-k+1n−k+1 follow from F\mathcal{F}F being a field.
  • Also works for F=C\mathcal {F}=\mathbb{C}F=C.

I. Cyclic Reed-Solomon codes over the complex numbers.

The following Reed-Solomon code over the complex numbers is called a cyclic code.

  • Let i=−1i=\sqrt{-1}i=−1​.
  • For j∈{0,⋯ ,n−1}j\in \{0,\cdots,n-1\}j∈{0,⋯,n−1}, choose aj=e2πij/na_j=e^{2\pi i j/n}aj​=e2πij/n. The aja_jaj​‘s are roots of unity of order nnn.
  • Use these aja_jaj​‘s to define a Reed-Solomon code as usual.

This code is cyclic:

  • Let c=(fc(a0),fc(a1),⋯ ,fc(an−1))c=(f_c(a_0),f_c(a_1),\cdots,f_c(a_{n-1}))c=(fc​(a0​),fc​(a1​),⋯,fc​(an−1​)) for some fc(x)∈C[x]f_c(x)\in \mathbb{C}[x]fc​(x)∈C[x].
  • Need to show that c′=fc(aj)c'=f_c(a_j)c′=fc​(aj​) for all j∈{0,⋯ ,n−1}j\in \{0,\cdots,n-1\}j∈{0,⋯,n−1}.
c′=(fc(a1),fc(a2),⋯ ,fc(an−1),fc(a0))=(fc(a0),fc(a1),⋯ ,fc(an−1))c'=(f_c(a_1),f_c(a_2),\cdots,f_c(a_{n-1}),f_c(a_0))=(f_c(a_0),f_c(a_1),\cdots,f_c(a_{n-1}))c′=(fc​(a1​),fc​(a2​),⋯,fc​(an−1​),fc​(a0​))=(fc​(a0​),fc​(a1​),⋯,fc​(an−1​))

II. Dual and Reversed Codes

  • Let C=[n,k,d]F\mathcal{C}=[n,k,d]_{\mathbb{F}}C=[n,k,d]F​ be an MDS code.

Definition for dual code of C\mathcal{C}C

The dual code of C\mathcal{C}C is

C⊥={c′∈Fn∣c′c⊤=0 for all c∈C}\mathcal{C}^\perp=\{c'\in \mathbb{F}^n|c'c^\top=0\text{ for all }c\in \mathcal{C}\}C⊥={c′∈Fn∣c′c⊤=0 for all c∈C}

Claim: C⊥\mathcal{C}^\perpC⊥ is an [n,n−k,k+1]F[n,n-k,k+1]_{\mathbb{F}}[n,n−k,k+1]F​ code.

Definition for reversed code of C\mathcal{C}C

The reversed code of C\mathcal{C}C is

CR={(cn−1,⋯ ,c0)∣(c0,⋯ ,cn−1)∈C}\mathcal{C}^R=\{(c_{n-1},\cdots,c_0)|(c_0,\cdots,c_{n-1})\in \mathcal{C}\}CR={(cn−1​,⋯,c0​)∣(c0​,⋯,cn−1​)∈C}

We claim that if C\mathcal{C}C is cyclic, then CR\mathcal{C}^RCR is cyclic.

III. Lemma about MDS codes

Let C=[n,k,n−k+1]F\mathcal{C}=[n,k,n-k+1]_{\mathbb{F}}C=[n,k,n−k+1]F​ be an MDS code.

Lemma

For any subset K⊂{0,⋯ ,n−1}\mathcal{K}\subset \{0,\cdots,n-1\}K⊂{0,⋯,n−1}, of size n−k+1n-k+1n−k+1 there exists c∈Cc\in \mathcal{C}c∈C whose support (set of nonzero indices) is K\mathcal{K}K.

Proof

Let G∈Fk×nG\in \mathbb{F}^{k\times n}G∈Fk×n be a generator matrix, and let GKc∈Fk×(k−1)G_{\mathcal{K}^c}\in \mathbb{F}^{k\times (k-1)}GKc​∈Fk×(k−1) be its restriction to columns not indexed by K\mathcal{K}K.

GKcG_{\mathcal{K}^c}GKc​ has more rows than columns, so there exists v∈Fkv\in \mathbb{F}^{k}v∈Fk such that vGKc=0vG_{\mathcal{K}^c}=0vGKc​=0.

So c=vGc=vGc=vG has at least ∣Kc∣=k−1|\mathcal{K}^c|=k-1∣Kc∣=k−1 zeros inn entires indexed by Kc\mathcal{K}^cKc.

The remaining n−(k−1)=dn-(k-1)=dn−(k−1)=d entries of ccc, indexed by K\mathcal{K}K, must be nonzero.

Thus the suppose of ccc is K\mathcal{K}K.

Construct gradient coding matrix

Consider nay nnn workers and sss stragglers.

Let d=s+1d=s+1d=s+1

Let C=[n,n−s]C\mathcal{C}=[n,n-s]_{\mathbb{C}}C=[n,n−s]C​ be the cyclic RS code build by I.

Then by III, there exists c∈cc\in \mathcal{c}c∈c whose support is the n−(n−s)+1=s+1n-(n-s)+1=s+1n−(n−s)+1=s+1 first entires.

Denote c=(β1,⋯ ,βs+1,0,0,⋯ ,0)c=(\beta_1,\cdots,\beta_{s+1},0,0,\cdots,0)c=(β1​,⋯,βs+1​,0,0,⋯,0). for some nonzero β1,⋯ ,βs+1\beta_1,\cdots,\beta_{s+1}β1​,⋯,βs+1​.

Build:

B∈Cn×nB\in \mathbb{C}^{n\times n}B∈Cn×n whose columns are all cyclic shifts of ccc.

We claim that BBB is a gradient coding matrix.

(β100βs+1βs⋯β2⋮β1⋮0βs+1⋮βs+1⋮⋮0βs+10βs+1⋱0⋮…0⋮0⋱β1⋮⋱⋮000βs+1βsβs−1⋯β1)\begin{pmatrix} \beta_1 & 0 & & 0 & \beta_{s+1} & \beta_s& \cdots & \beta_2 \\ \vdots & \beta_1 & & \vdots & 0 & \beta_{s+1} & & \vdots \\ \beta_{s+1} & \vdots & & & \vdots & 0 & & \beta_{s+1}\\ 0 & \beta_{s+1} & \ddots & 0 & &\vdots & \dots & 0\\ \vdots & 0 & \ddots & \beta_1 & & & & &\\ & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & & & &0\\ 0 & 0 & & \beta_{s+1}& \beta_s& \beta_{s-1}& \cdots & \beta_1\\ \end{pmatrix}​β1​⋮βs+1​0⋮0​0β1​⋮βs+1​0⋮0​⋱⋱⋱​0⋮0β1​⋮βs+1​​βs+1​0⋮βs​​βs​βs+1​0⋮βs−1​​⋯…⋯​β2​⋮βs+1​00β1​​​​

Proof

Every row is a codeword in CR\mathcal{C}^RCR.

  • Specifically, a shift of (0,⋯ ,0,βs+1,⋯ ,β1)(0,\cdots,0,\beta_{s+1},\cdots,\beta_1)(0,⋯,0,βs+1​,⋯,β1​).
  • Then every row contains ≤d=s+1\leq d=s+1≤d=s+1 nonzeros.

I\mathcal{I}I is in the span of any n−sn-sn−s rows of BBB.

  • Observe that I∈C\mathcal{I}\in \mathcal{C}I∈C, (evaluate the polynomial f(x)=1f(x)=1f(x)=1 at α1,⋯ ,αn\alpha_1,\cdots,\alpha_nα1​,⋯,αn​).
  • Then I∈CR\mathcal{I}\in \mathcal{C}^RI∈CR.
  • Therefore, it suffices to show that any n−sn-sn−s span CR\mathcal{C}^RCR.
  • Since dim⁡C=dim⁡CR=n−s\dim \mathcal{C}=\dim \mathcal{C}^R=n-sdimC=dimCR=n−s, it suffices to show that any n−sn-sn−s rows are independent.

Observe: The left most n−sn-sn−s columns are linearly independent, and therefore span C\mathcal{C}C.

Assume for contradiction there exists n−sn-sn−s dependent rows.

Then ∃v∈Cn\exists v\in \mathbb{C}^{n}∃v∈Cn such that vB=0vB=0vB=0.

vvv is orthogonal to the basis of C\mathcal{C}C.

So v∈C⊥v\in \mathcal{C}^\perpv∈C⊥.

From II, C⊥\mathcal{C}^\perpC⊥ is an [n,s][n,s][n,s] MDS code, and hence every v∈C⊥v\in \mathcal{C}^\perpv∈C⊥ is of Hamming weight ≥n−s+1\geq n-s+1≥n−s+1.

This is a contradiction.

Bound for gradient coding

We want sss to be large and ddd to be small.

How small can ddd with respect to sss?

  • A: Build a bipartite graph.
    • Left side: nnn workers W1,⋯ ,WnW_1,\cdots,W_nW1​,⋯,Wn​.
    • Right side: nnn partial datasets D1,⋯ ,DnD_1,\cdots,D_nD1​,⋯,Dn​.
    • Connect WiW_iWi​ to DiD_iDi​ if worker iii contains DiD_iDi​.
      • Equivalently if Bi,i≠0B_{i,i}\neq 0Bi,i​=0.
    • deg⁡(Wi)=d\deg (W_i) = ddeg(Wi​)=d by definition.
    • deg⁡(Dj)≥s+1\deg (\mathcal{D}_j)\geq s+1deg(Dj​)≥s+1.
    • Sum degree on left ndndnd and right ≥n(s+1)\geq n(s+1)≥n(s+1).
    • So d≥s+1d\geq s+1d≥s+1.

We can break the lower bound using approximate computation.
